variables.content: For stoichiometric YbRh2Si2 the onset of lattice Kondo correlations is detectable around the coherence scale $T_{\rm coh}\approx T_{\rm K}\approx25$–30 K (the effective single‑ion Kondo or transport coherence temperature extracted from bulk probes), yet the lattice Kondo correlations reach a regime in which they dominate low‑energy electronic properties only below the crossover temperature $T_{\mathrm{P}}\approx3.3\ \mathrm{K}$, i.e., at $T_{\mathrm{P}\!}\approx 0.11\cdot T_{\rm coh}\approx 0.1\,T_{\rm coh}$; consequently, single‑ion Kondo effects persist down to temperatures well below $T_{\rm coh}$ before lattice coherence contributions grow sufficiently to enable the non‑Fermi‑liquid quantum‑critical behavior observed in bulk and spectroscopic probes.

The renormalized-band calculation exhibits a quasiparticle spectral weight associated with the narrow Kondo-derived f-electron peak that decreases smoothly with increasing magnetic field B (the computed peak amplitude and derived low-energy effective-mass indicators vary continuously and only gradually across the ≈10 T scale); under the assumption that the renormalized-band treatment captures the essential field-dependent many-body physics responsible for Kondo screening in the periodic lattice, this calculated smooth decrease is taken to imply that in the real material YbRh2Si2 the Kondo-singlet-derived quasiparticle weight is suppressed by a smooth crossover as a function of B rather than by an abrupt, discontinuous (first-order) transition at that field scale.

The combination of experimental facts — (i) the presence of a strongly anisotropic and persistent ESR line in YbRh2Si2, (ii) a g-factor whose temperature and magnetic-field dependences mirror the temperature- and field-dependent Sommerfeld coefficient γ = C_el/T and the 29Si NMR Knight shift and 1/(T1T), and (iii) a linewidth that acquires a T² low-temperature behaviour together with a field-dependent residual broadening — demonstrates that the observed ESR in YbRh2Si2 is a resonance excitation of the coherent heavy quasiparticles that form below the single-ion Kondo (spin-fluctuation) scale T_K ≃ T_0 ≈ 25 K. Consequently, the ESR parameters (the resonance field B_res or equivalently the resonance g, and the linewidth ΔB) are governed by the field- and temperature-dependent quasiparticle effective mass m*(B,T), the quasiparticle density of states at the Fermi level N(E_F;B,T), and the quasiparticle spin relaxation times; therefore ESR provides direct experimental access to the dynamics and evolution of the Kondo state and to the crossover between non–Fermi-liquid and Landau Fermi-liquid regimes in this Kondo-lattice system.

A fermion‑condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT) is a zero‑temperature quantum critical point occurring at a critical value x=x_{FC} of a nonthermal control parameter x (for example an effective itinerant‑electron density) at which the single‑particle spectrum develops a momentum interval with an essentially dispersionless (flat) energy and the quasiparticle effective mass M^{*} diverges; here M^{*} denotes the quasiparticle mass characterizing the low‑energy density of states and quasiparticle dispersion. The presence of such an FCQPT inside an AF ordered region implies that the flat band and divergent M^{*} are the underlying origin of the experimentally observed crossover line T*(B) at elevated temperatures: an applied magnetic field B polarizes quasiparticles (via the Zeeman energy μ_B B, where μ_B is the Bohr magneton), destroys or reduces the flat band, and thereby generates an energy scale T*_{FC}(B) proportional to μ_B B (T*_{FC}(B)=const·μ_B B) which controls the NFL–LFL crossover at temperatures above the AF ordering scale. Consequently, FCQPT acts as the quantum critical point that underlies the similar T–B crossover phenomenology seen in the experimentally measured phase diagrams of YbRh2Si2 and its chemically tuned variants Yb(Rh0.93Co0.07)2Si2 and Yb(Rh0.94Ir0.06)2Si2.

The combined phenomenology of YbRh2Si2 in magnetic field is explained by the interplay of three microscopic ingredients: (a) the crystalline-electric-field (CEF) ground-state symmetry of the Yb3+ ion that produces a strongly anisotropic 4f–conduction hybridization matrix element and an enhanced anisotropy of the quasiparticle effective g factor, (b) a smooth, field-driven suppression of local Kondo screening that continuously reduces the quasiparticle effective mass m*(B) and modifies the amplitude of the Kondo-derived coherence peak (manifested as a field-dependent Sommerfeld–Wilson ratio and a moderate continuous reduction of the electronic Sommerfeld coefficient γ(B)), and (c) coherence effects intrinsic to the periodic Kondo lattice that create sharp van Hove–type structures (flattened dispersions or peaks) in the renormalized quasiparticle DOS so that Zeeman spin-splitting moves these DOS features through the Fermi energy and produces successive Lifshitz transitions; this combined mechanism accounts quantitatively for why distinct Lifshitz transitions appear at fields of order the Kondo scale, why the effective mass diminishes continuously while transport and thermodynamic observables show abrupt changes at specific fields, and why multiple transitions (three in these experiments) can occur instead of a single metamagnetic jump.

Using the assignments $T_{\rm coh}\approx T_{\rm K}\approx 25$–30 K (the effective single‑ion Kondo/coherence scale extracted from bulk probes) and the experimentally extracted crossover temperature $T_{\mathrm{P}}\approx 3.3\ \mathrm{K}$ at which pronounced lattice‑Kondo‑dominated spectral and transport signatures appear, the numerical ratio $T_{\mathrm{P}}/T_{\rm coh}\approx 0.11$ (approximately $0.1$) follows for the studied stoichiometric YbRh2Si2 samples; this numerical hierarchy (dominance of lattice Kondo correlations only below $\sim0.1\cdot T_{\rm coh}$) is meaningful for interpreting the temperature evolution in this material, while recognizing that disorder, sample dependence, surface effects, and other material‑specific microscopic parameters may alter this numerical ratio in other systems.
      Köhler, U. et al., Phys. Rev. B 77, 104412 (2008) (empirical $T_K$ references used by the authors).

For the heavy‑fermion compound YbRh_2Si_2, using the FCQPT framework together with the universal normalized effective‑mass function $M_N^*(y)$ and empirical internal scales $M_M^*(B)=\\max_T\\{C(T,B)/T\\}$ and $T_M(B)$ yields a quantitative collapse of measured specific‑heat‑coefficient curves $C(T,B)/T$ at different magnetic fields B onto the theoretical $M_N^*(y)$: after normalization each experimental curve exhibits the low‑y LFL plateau, a crossover peak at $y\\sim1$ corresponding to the maximum $M_M^*(B)$ whose position shifts with magnetic field in proportion to $B-B_{c0}$, and the high‑y NFL decay consistent with $M^*\\propto T^{-2/3}$, so that the FCQPT‑based scaling accounts for the field dependence of both the peak magnitudes $M_M^*(B)$ and the extended temperature range (up to ~20 K) over which NFL behavior is observed experimentally for YbRh_2Si_2.

Comparison of experimentally measured exponents and crossover laws for effective-mass-sensitive observables in heavy-fermion compounds with the scaling predictions of the two FCQPT-based scenarios provides a classification consistent with the FCQPT framework: materials such as CeNi2Ge2, CeCoIn5, and YbAgGe display behavior consistent with the highly correlated (pre-FCQPT) scenario (notably $M^{*}(T)\propto T^{-1/2}$ and $M^{*}(B)\propto(B-B_{c0})^{-2/3}$), whereas YbRh2(Si0.95Ge0.05)2 and YbRh2Si2 display behavior consistent with the strongly correlated (post-FCQPT) scenario (notably $M^{*}(T)\propto1/T$ and $M^{*}(B)\propto(B-B_{c0})^{-1/2}$); specific measured quantities such as specific-heat exponents, Grüneisen divergence, magnetoresistance sign changes, and magnetization scalings are in qualitative and, in many cases, quantitative agreement with these derived laws.

The maximum value of the quasiparticle effective mass extracted from the temperature dependence of the specific-heat coefficient in YbRh$_{2}$Si$_{2}$ follows the scaling law
      $$
      M_{\max}^{*}(B)\propto \frac{d}{\sqrt{B-B_{c0}}},
      $$
      where $B_{c0}$ is the magnetic-field value that tunes the system to the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT), $d$ is a field-independent fitting constant, and the proportionality is taken to hold over the experimental field window considered; using $B_{c0}\simeq 0.06\ \mathrm{T}$ for YbRh$_{2}$Si$_{2}$ produces quantitative agreement with the measured maxima of $C/T$ as a function of $B$.

The fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT) theoretical framework, based on a quasiparticle paradigm in which the effective mass becomes a function of temperature and of a tuning parameter (density or magnetic field), is assumed to capture the dominant low-temperature thermodynamic scaling phenomena in both (a) two-dimensional neutral $^{3}$He films where atoms interact via van-der-Waals forces with strong hard-core repulsion and a large bare mass, and (b) three-dimensional heavy-fermion metals where itinerant electrons are subject to Kondo-lattice physics; this proposition asserts that despite these microscopic differences, the FCQPT framework provides the correct low-energy fixed-point-like description controlling the observed universal scaling.
      [9]
